---
title: "Common-effect and random-effects models"
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{common-effect-random-effect}
  %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

## Introduction

The most widely used meta-analysis models are the **common-effect** and
**random-effects** models.

The common-effect model assumes all studies estimate the same true effect,
while the random-effects model allows the true effect to vary across studies.

These models form the foundation for most extensions implemented in **bayesma**.

## Common-effect model

Let

- \(y_i\) = effect estimate from study \(i\)
- \(s_i\) = standard error of the estimate

The sampling model is

\[
y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, s_i^2)
\]

where \(\theta\) is the shared effect.

A prior is placed on the effect:

\[
\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\theta^2)
\]

### Interpretation

All studies are assumed to estimate the same underlying effect.

## Random-effects model

The random-effects model introduces heterogeneity between studies.

\[
y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_i, s_i^2)
\]

\[
\theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)
\]

where

- \(\mu\) = overall effect
- \(\tau\) = between-study heterogeneity

### Assumptions

- study effects are exchangeable
- heterogeneity follows a normal distribution
- sampling errors are known
